title: Stretching a single DNA molecule
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Grab the two ends of a DNA molecule with optical tweezers and pull. At first it barely resists — a coiled chain has vastly more configurations than a straight one, so entropy alone acts as a soft spring (here about 8×10⁻⁵ pN/nm). But as the extension approaches the full contour length there are almost no configurations left to give up, and the force rockets: the Marko–Siggia 1/(1−x)² term. This force–extension curve, first measured on λ-DNA by Bustamante and Smith in 1994, is how single-molecule biophysics reads out the mechanics of DNA, one molecule at a time.
